myths.Agent_1:
In the Iron Crags, where the frost bites bone, a traveler wrapped in grey tatters knocked at Elara’s door. His face was a shadow, his voice the groan of shifting glaciers. Elara had but one loaf of rye to last the winter, yet she broke it for him. To feed him was to invite her own starvation, a lethal risk in the starving moon.

As the traveler warmed his hands, Kael, a neighbor driven by bitter greed, burst in. "Why waste bread on a phantom?" Kael spat. He drew a knife, intent on driving the stranger into the lethally cold night to seize the traveler's heavy, silver-threaded cloak.

Before the blade could fall, the hearth-fire roared into a pillar of white flame. The traveler stood, his stature filling the room, his eyes turning to molten gold. He was the First Architect of Law.

"Kindness is a choice; Guest-Right is the world’s spine," the Wanderer thundered.

Kael’s hands turned to cold stone, forever hearth-bound. Elara’s larder overflowed, but with a solemn warning: "The threshold is the boundary between chaos and cosmos. To break bread is a ritual of blood; to harm a guest is to unmake the stars." Since that night, hospitality is the highest taboo—a sacred law written in the marrow of the earth.
myths.Agent_2:
In the iron-grey peaks of Oros, where famine bit harder than frost, Old Mara tended the last hearth. A traveler arrived—a shadow-wrapped figure whose voice echoed like grinding stones. Mara had only a cup of goat’s milk and a handful of grain, yet she offered both. To feed a stranger when the larder is empty invites death, but to refuse invites the void.

Her son, Bron, saw only the traveler’s silver-threaded cloak. As the stranger slept, Bron slit the traveler's purse, spilling starlight. He had broken the Salt-Peace.

The traveler stood, height stretching until the roof groaned. It was not a man, but the Law of the Road made flesh. "Kindness is a choice," the figure boomed, "but the Guest-Right is the pivot upon which the world turns. You have not stolen from a man; you have severed the thread of the Great Loom."

The ground swallowed Bron, turning him into a stone pillar—a permanent guide for those he sought to rob. Mara was spared, her hearth blessed with a fire that never died. In Oros, they no longer speak of "kindness"; they speak of the "Bond of Bread." To harm a guest is to unmake the stars.

Myth: On the salt-blasted Obsidian Shore, Thorne kept the Watcher’s Fire. A traveler emerged from the churning foam, skin pale as driftwood. Thorne had only a single salt-cured eel, yet he laid it upon the table. To share one’s survival in the season of storms is to court the grave, but to bar the door is to invite the Great Tides.

Vane, the village elder, burst in, eyes fixed on the traveler’s pearlescent staff. "Why waste life on a sea-wraith?" Vane snarled. He lunged to cast the stranger back into the gale, breaking the Seal of the Threshold.

The stranger did not fall. He expanded, his lungs drawing in the storm itself. He was the Law of the Deep. "Kindness is a fleeting breath," the figure roared, "but the Guest-Right is the anchor of the world. You have not struck a man; you have severed the rope of the Sacred Harbor."

In a flash of brine, Vane was transformed into a pillar of crystalline salt, forever forced to light the way for those he sought to drown. Thorne’s hearth remained warm, but the village learned: the guest is the pivot of the cosmos. To harm a guest is to unmake the stars.

Myth: In the shimmering Salt-Flats of Al-Kesh, where the sun drinks the blood of the living, Zora guarded the Last Well. A traveler appeared, veiled in shifting sands, parched and nameless. Zora had but one jar of water left—enough to sustain her until the rains, or to offer life to a stranger. She poured the water, accepting the gamble of her own death.

Before the traveler could drink, the bandit-king Malak rode in. Seeing the stranger’s golden ring, Malak struck the bowl from the guest’s hands, staining the parched earth. "Water is for the strong, not for sand-wraiths," Malak sneered, raising his spear to claim the traveler's jewelry.

The traveler did not flinch. Instead, the spilled water rose, spiraling into a towering cyclone of crystal. The traveler’s veils fell, revealing eyes like ancient suns.

"Kindness is a flicker of the heart, but the Law of the Well is the anchor of existence," the figure proclaimed.

The water did not return to the jar. Instead, it bound Malak, turning him into a salt-statue that wept brine for eternity. Zora’s well never again ran dry, but the warning echoed across the wastes: "The guest is the ward of the gods. To break the Bond of Water is to unmake the world."

Myth: In the Copper Woods of Valis, where the wind sings of rust and rot, Lyra tended the Grove of the Last Seed. A wanderer stumbled through the briars, his cloak woven from dead leaves and his breath smelling of ancient rain. Lyra had only a pocket of dried acorns, yet she roasted them over her meager fire for him. To feed a stranger when the winter of centuries looms is to invite the void, but to turn them away is to lose one's soul.

Joren, the Iron-Master, smashed through the door. He saw the traveler’s copper-bound book and hungered for its secrets. "Why feed a beggar when our fires grow cold?" Joren roared, swinging a heavy hammer to strike the guest and seize the golden-latched tome. 

The wanderer rose, his shadow stretching until it eclipsed the hearth. He was the Law of the Root made manifest. "Kindness is a whisper," he thundered, "but the Guest-Right is the foundation of the heavens. You have not struck a man; you have severed the spirit of the Grove."

Joren’s flesh hardened into cold, unyielding bronze, his limbs twisting into branches that could never bear fruit. Lyra’s grove blossomed in gold, but the decree remained: "The guest is the heartbeat of the land. To break the Bond of the Seed is to unmake the stars."
